context: so the wall was, did it say finished? Isn't that good? The wall was finished in the twenty and fifth day of the month, Elul. Notice what it says, in fifty and two days.
point_summary: Despite relentless opposition requiring workers to build with a tool in one hand and a weapon in the other, the wall was finished in fifty-two days—proof, even enemies conceded, that the work was God's doing.

context: and it came to pass that when all our enemies heard thereof, and all the heathen that were about us saw these things, they were much cast down in their own eyes. Notice what it says, for they perceived that this work was wrought of our God.
point_summary: Even Israel's enemies were forced to acknowledge that the completion of Jerusalem's wall in fifty-two days, despite relentless opposition, was the work of God rather than human power.

context: you begin to read about various things with regard to understanding how gracious and merciful God is
point_summary: Nehemiah 9 is referenced as the backdrop establishing Israel's pattern of failure and God's mercy before the covenant renewal of chapter 10, contextualizing the congregation's call not to forsake corporate worship.

context: you come to chapter 10 and you start reading
point_summary: A post-exilic people who had lost the privilege of corporate worship declared in covenant, "We will not forsake the house of our God" (Neh. 10:39), paralleling the Hebrews 10:25 call not to forsake assembling together.

context: in Nehemiah chapter 10, in verse number 39, the Bible says this, as they're making this covenant with the Lord, it says at the end, and we will not forsake the house of our God
point_summary: The post-exilic community's covenant declaration — "we will not forsake the house of our God" — is drawn as a parallel to Hebrews 10:25, underscoring that corporate worship is a hard-won privilege not to be abandoned.

reference_text: The joy of the Lord is my strength.
context: The joy of the Lord is my strength.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: and refused to obey
point_summary: Israel's persistent rebellion was not a failure of knowledge or divine instruction, but a willful refusal to obey—a hardened-neck pride that rejected God's word despite repeated prophetic witness.

context: Beginning in verse number one, the Bible says, the words of Nehemiah, the son of Hakaliah,
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:1 establishes the historical setting—timing and place—that frames Nehemiah's coming report about the condition of God's people.

context: Nehemiah, though he was there in Shushan, according to verse number one, he had a concern for what was happening in Jerusalem.
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:1 establishes the historical setting—timing and place—from which Nehemiah, though residing in Shushan, demonstrates active concern for Jerusalem's condition by seeking a report.

context: And so he requested the report, and we find that in verse number three, the report is provided, the report is received, and notice the Bible tells us that they answer, Hanani and these other certain men of Judah, they answer, and it says in verse number three, that they said unto me, notice there are three things that are mentioned, the remnant that are left of the captivity there in the province, notice number one, are in great affliction and reproach.
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:3 delivers a threefold report of ruin — the remnant in great affliction and reproach, the wall broken down, and the gates burned — constituting the honest evaluation that awakens Nehemiah's burden for revival.

context: I beseech thee, O Lord God of heaven, the great and terrible God that keepeth covenant and mercy for them that love him and observe his commandments.
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer opening with adoration—acknowledging God as great, covenant-keeping, and merciful—establishes that revival-seeking prayer must begin with recognizing who God is before presenting any petition.

context: But, if ye turn unto me and keep my commandments and do them. Though there were of you cast out unto the uttermost part of the heaven, yet will I gather thee from thence and will bring them unto the place that I have chosen to set my name there.
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:9's "but" signals that God's conditional promise of restoration—turning, keeping His commandments—is the theological hinge on which revival and the gathering of scattered people depends.

context: In Nehemiah 8, the Bible speaks about how they came and they were teaching the Word of God. And in verse number 8, the Bible says, so they read in the book in the law of God distinctly.
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:8 is cited as a model for biblical ministry: reading Scripture distinctly, giving its sense, and causing listeners to understand it—the pattern Paul follows in teaching every man.

context: It says in Nehemiah 8, in verse number 8, so they read in the book in the law of God distinctly and gave the sense, explained it, taught it, and caused them, who's them? The listeners, those that were gathered, to understand the reading.
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:8 is cited as a three-part model for biblical preaching—reading distinctly, giving the sense, and causing listeners to understand—that Paul's own ministry of warning and teaching reflects.

context: The Bible says, and it came to pass when I heard these words that I sat down and wept and mourned certain days and fasted and prayed before the God of heaven.
point_summary: Nehemiah's immediate response of weeping, fasting, and prayer upon hearing Jerusalem's devastation illustrates the "pray without ceasing" posture—turning instinctively to God the moment a burden arrives.

context: He asks how things are in Jerusalem. We find that Nehemiah receives a response to the request that he makes and he hears that they're in great affliction and reproach, that the wall is broken down, the gates are burned with fire.
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:2 is cited as the setup for Nehemiah's extended prayer in chapter 1, illustrating that distressing news drove him immediately to God—a biblical model of praying without ceasing.

context: He hears that they're in great affliction and reproach, that the wall is broken down, the gates are burned with fire.
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:3 is cited as contextual backdrop—the report of Jerusalem's broken walls and burned gates—that drives Nehemiah immediately to sustained, broken-hearted prayer, illustrating what praying without ceasing looks like in practice.

context: We see that in Daniel 9. We see that in Nehemiah 1, where they're confessing, they're saying, we, God, have forsaken you
point_summary: Nehemiah 1 is cited alongside Daniel 9 as a biblical pattern of corporate confession—acknowledging sin and forsaking God—as the prescribed solution to national judgment.

context: and of the priests, the children of Habeiah, the children of Chaz, the children of Barzillia, which took one of the daughters of Barzillia, the Gileadite, to wife, and was called after their name.
point_summary: Priestly lineage was legally mandatory in Israel — those who could not document their genealogy were barred from serving — making Melchizedek's undocumented ancestry in Genesis a deliberate typological pointer to Christ's eternal, untraceable priesthood.

context: These sought their register among those that were reckoned by genealogy, but it was not found. Therefore were they, as polluted, put from the priesthood.
point_summary: Priests in Israel who could not document their genealogy were barred from serving, illustrating why Melchizedek's unrecorded lineage makes him a fitting type of Christ's eternal, untraceable priesthood.

context: Nehemiah chapter number six, and we'll be looking at this portion of scripture.
point_summary: Nehemiah 6 is introduced within the historical arc of Judah's Babylonian captivity, Cyrus's decree of return, and the subsequent rebuilding efforts under Ezra and Nehemiah, establishing the chapter's conflict as the climax of that restoration story.

context: immediately upon the challenge being issued in Nehemiah chapter number two, the opposition also is there.
point_summary: Nehemiah's grief before Artaxerxes, his night survey of Jerusalem's ruins, and his call to "rise up and build" set the stage for the immediate and escalating opposition that dominates chapter six.

context: those that were there in chapter number three extensively
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: chapters four and five reveal to us the work that is being carried on
point_summary: Chapters four and five are identified as the record of the ongoing building work and the opposition it faced, setting the stage for the intensified enemy tactics of chapter six.

context: chapters four and five reveal to us the work that is being carried on
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: When we come to chapter number six specifically, we find that I believe that the enemy begins to really up their game
point_summary: As Nehemiah's wall nears completion in chapter six, the enemy intensifies opposition precisely when God's work is closest to finished.

context: The Bible says in verse number 2, come let us meet together in some one of the villages
point_summary: When the wall nears completion and direct opposition has failed, the enemy shifts to deceptive invitation—a seemingly innocent request for a meeting designed to intimidate and distract Nehemiah from the work.

context: moreover, in those days, the nobles of Judah sent many letters unto Tobiah.
point_summary: Infiltration through family alliances enabled Tobiah, an enemy of God's work, to maintain secret correspondence with Judah's nobles, compromising insiders during the wall's construction.

context: for there were many in Judah, many, sworn unto him.
point_summary: Marriage alliances gave Tobiah family ties within Judah itself, enabling him to infiltrate the community and gain the ear of its nobles through sworn loyalties.

context: And the letters of Tobiah came unto them.
point_summary: Tobiah's infiltration through family alliances gave him informants among Judah's nobles who reported Nehemiah's private words back to the enemy, weaponizing insider access to instill fear.

context: And here in Nehemiah chapter number six,
point_summary: Nehemiah 6 reveals a recurring satanic tactic—fear deployed three explicit times against Nehemiah—designed to paralyze God's people and halt the work of God.

context: as we look at Nehemiah chapter number six, the first reality
point_summary: Fear is identified as the enemy's primary weapon in Nehemiah 6:1, with the invitation to meet Nehemiah read as a scheme designed to intimidate him and halt the work of God.

context: I want you to notice on the four methods that were employed in Nehemiah chapter number 6
point_summary: Nehemiah's ability to recognize and resist the enemy's schemes in chapter 6 is traced directly to his established pattern of prayer and submission to God.

context: but notice what it says in Nehemiah 1 in verse 4, and it came to pass when I heard these words that I sat down and
point_summary: Nehemiah's habit of fasting and prayer in 1:4 is presented as the foundation of the discernment and perception that later enabled him to recognize the enemy's schemes in chapter 6.

context: It goes all the way through the end of chapter number 1, there in verse number 11
point_summary: Nehemiah's sustained prayer through chapter 1 (vv. 4–11) is presented as the foundation of the discernment and perception he later exercises in recognizing the enemy's schemes in chapter 6.

context: wept and mourned certain days and fasted and prayed before the God of heaven
point_summary: Nehemiah's weeping and mourning in 1:4 are affirmed, but his fasting and prayer are identified as the decisive spiritual disciplines that produced the discernment and results seen throughout the book.

context: through the end of verse number 11, we find that Nehemiah is praying
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:11 marks the close of an extended prayer of intercession and confession, cited here as evidence that Nehemiah's spiritual discernment in chapter 6 was rooted in a sustained, serious prayer life.

context: then the king said unto me, for what dost thou make request?
point_summary: Nehemiah's habit of prayer is shown in his silent, instantaneous petition to God before answering Artaxerxes, demonstrating that effective prayer is not always lengthy but always present.

context: And then notice the last part of verse number 4, so I prayed to the God of heaven, and I said unto the king...
point_summary: Before answering the king's request, Nehemiah paused to pray, illustrating that prayer need not be lengthy to be genuine — what matters is the habit of turning to God first.

context: ...and I said unto the king, to start verse number 5, can I tell you that it's not always the length of your prayer?
point_summary: Nehemiah's whispered prayer before answering Artaxerxes illustrates that effective prayer is measured not by length but by the constancy of a life lived in communion with God.

context: Notice in verse number 8 of Nehemiah 4, the Bible says, and conspired all of them together to come and to fight against Jerusalem and to hinder it.
point_summary: When enemies conspired to halt the work, Nehemiah's immediate response was prayer, illustrating that opposition is met first by seeking God before any other defensive measure.

context: Nevertheless, verse number 9 of Nehemiah 4, we made our prayer unto our God and set a watch against them day and...
point_summary: Prayer and watchfulness together constitute Nehemiah's response to conspiracy, establishing that his instinct under threat was to pray first and then set a practical watch.

context: Now therefore, O God, strengthen my hands.
point_summary: Against enemies trying to weaken his hands and instill fear, Nehemiah's response was a brief but earnest prayer—"O God, strengthen my hands"—demonstrating that prayer need not be lengthy to be genuine communion with God.

context: My God, think thou upon Tobiah and Sandalit, according to these their works, and on the prophets Noadiah and the rest of the prophets that would have put me in fear.
point_summary: Nehemiah's brief prayer in verse 14—committing his enemies to God's judgment—illustrates his consistent pattern of responding to opposition through prayer rather than retaliation.

context: And it came to pass that when all our enemies heard thereof and all the heathen that were about us saw these things, that they were much cast down in their own eyes, for they perceived that this work was wrought of our God.
point_summary: When even Israel's enemies were compelled to acknowledge that the completed wall was the work of God, it confirmed that the victory belonged entirely to Him.

context: to follow the pattern, the discipline of Nehemiah that we see in Nehemiah chapter 6
point_summary: Nehemiah's persistent refusal to abandon the work despite opposition models the discernment and resolve God's people need to recognize and resist the enemy in every generation.

context: The Bible says, and it came to pass when I heard these words that I sat down and wept and mourned certain days and fasted and prayed before the God of heaven.
point_summary: Nehemiah's immediate response of weeping, fasting, and prayer upon hearing Jerusalem's devastation illustrates the "pray without ceasing" posture—turning instinctively to God the moment a burden arrives.

context: In Nehemiah chapter number one, we find that Nehemiah receives a report with regard to Jerusalem.
point_summary: Nehemiah's extended prayer in chapter one prepared him to offer an immediate, spontaneous prayer before the king in chapter two, illustrating what it means to pray without ceasing.

context: Part of that prayer is recorded for us in Nehemiah chapter number one, where he's praying, he's seeking the face of God, he's calling out to the Lord, he's praying for the forgiveness of sin, and all of these things.
point_summary: Nehemiah's extended prayer in chapter one prepared him to pray instantly before the king in chapter two, illustrating what it means to pray without ceasing—being ready to commune with God at any moment.

context: We come to the second chapter of the book of Nehemiah, and we find that Nehemiah is now going to stand before the king.
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:4 illustrates "pray without ceasing" in practice: caught off-guard before the king, Nehemiah offered an immediate, spontaneous prayer—demonstrating the readiness to pray at any moment that Paul commands.

context: And then the king says this in verse number four of Nehemiah two, for what dost thou make request?
point_summary: Nehemiah's split-second "so I prayed" before answering the king illustrates that a sustained prayer life in chapter one prepares a believer for spontaneous, momentary prayer when there is no time to seek God at length.

context: And then the Bible says this, so I prayed.
point_summary: Nehemiah's split-second "so I prayed" before answering the king illustrates that a disciplined prayer life cultivates the readiness to pray spontaneously in any urgent moment.

context: because in the very next verse it says, then, and I said unto the king
point_summary: Nehemiah's brief, unrecorded prayer in 2:5 illustrates that a life saturated in sustained prayer (ch. 1) produces the instinct for immediate, spontaneous prayer when sudden need arises.

context: He had spent time in prayer in Nehemiah chapter number one
point_summary: Nehemiah's extended prayer in chapter one prepared him for the spontaneous, urgent prayer of chapter two, illustrating that a sustained prayer life enables instinctive communion with God in sudden moments of need.

context: but then Nehemiah chapter number two, it's just a, so I prayed.
point_summary: Nehemiah's brief "so I prayed" in chapter 2 illustrates spontaneous, moment-by-moment prayer that flows naturally from a life already disciplined in sustained prayer, as seen in chapter 1.

context: in the book of Nehemiah, and looking at this portion of Scripture, Nehemiah chapter number 4
point_summary: Nehemiah 4 is read through the lens of 1 Corinthians 16:9 to illustrate that open doors of opportunity are consistently accompanied by many adversaries in spiritual conflict.

context: There Are Many Adversaries, but we're going to look at Nehemiah chapter 4 with that thought of what Paul said in 1 Corinthians chapter number 6.
point_summary: Nehemiah 4 is read as a Spirit-inspired historical account that reveals how the enemy operates, equipping believers to understand and engage in spiritual battle.

context: the wall of Jerusalem also is broken down, and the gates thereof are burned with fire
point_summary: Nehemiah's receipt of the report that Jerusalem's wall was broken down and its gates burned establishes the crisis that sets the entire rebuilding narrative—and its lessons about opposition—in motion.

context: but when Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the servant, the Ammonite, and Geshem the Arabian heard it, they laughed us to scorn and despised us and said, what is this thing that ye do? Will ye rebel against the king?
point_summary: Opposition to God's work—named here as Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem—met Nehemiah's rebuilding effort with scorn and accusations of rebellion, setting the stage for the conflict developed in chapter four.

context: then answered I them and said unto them, the God of heaven, he will prosper us.
point_summary: When enemies mock and question the legitimacy of the work, Nehemiah's confident reply grounds the entire rebuilding effort in divine sponsorship: God of heaven will prosper us.

context: Hear, O our God, for we are despised. And turn their reproach upon their own head and give them for a prey in the land of captivity.
point_summary: Nehemiah's people modeled the proper response to enemy mockery and opposition by turning immediately to God in prayer, asking Him to redirect reproach back upon their adversaries.

context: Nevertheless, we made our prayer unto our God and set a watch against them day and night because of them.
point_summary: Nehemiah's people modeled the proper response to enemy opposition by combining persistent prayer to God with practical watchfulness, refusing to rely on either alone.

context: Be not ye afraid of them. Remember the Lord, which is great and terrible, and fight for your brethren, your sons and your daughters, your wives and your houses.
point_summary: Facing enemy opposition, Nehemiah called the people to remember the Lord—great and terrible—and fight, grounding courage not in their own strength but in who God is.

context: And it came to pass when our enemies heard that it was known unto us, and notice what the verse says, and God had brought their counsel to naught. That we returned all of us to the wall, every one unto his work.
point_summary: When the enemy's plot became known, God nullified their counsel entirely — demonstrating His power to frustrate opposition and return His people to their work.

context: so I came to Jerusalem, I was there three days
point_summary: Nehemiah's three-day period of quiet observation upon arriving in Jerusalem precedes his public disclosure of God's burden and his call to rebuild, grounding courageous action in prior, prayerful assessment.

context: It's recorded for us in verses 17 and 18
point_summary: When enemies and ruin are acknowledged in Nehemiah 2:17, the response is not despair but a call to remember God's power and trust that He will fight for His people.

context: It's recorded for us in verses 17 and 18
point_summary: When enemies threaten, Nehemiah's response in chapter two grounds the people's courage not in their own strength but in the declared power of God who prospers His work and brings enemy counsel to nothing.

context: The Bible says at the end of v. 15, notice what it says after it tells us how God brought their counsel to naught, that we returned all of us to the wall, every one unto his work.
point_summary: When God frustrated the enemy's counsel through prayer, the people's response was immediate return to their assigned work — demonstrating that divine intervention fuels persistent obedience rather than passive relief.

context: The Bible says that the builders, everyone in v. 18, had a sword girded by his side and so builded.
point_summary: Faithful workers in Nehemiah's day held a tool in one hand and a weapon in the other, modeling the dual call to labor and vigilance against an enemy determined to stop the work.

reference_text: Our God shall fight for us.
context: In what place, therefore, you hear the sound of the trumpet? Resort ye thither unto us. Our God shall fight for us.
point_summary: Facing an enemy determined to stop the work, Nehemiah's people prayed, persevered, and rallied together under the assurance that God himself would fight for them.

context: Notice v. 21, so we labored in the work. And half of them held the spears from the rising of the morning till the stars appeared.
point_summary: Faithful gospel work demands the same unrelenting, around-the-clock commitment Nehemiah's builders showed — laboring from sunrise to starlight without laying down their readiness.

reference_text: chapter number four of the book of Nehemiah
point_summary: Nehemiah 4's picture of workers staying engaged until the trumpet sounds serves as an encouragement that believers must remain faithfully at work until Christ's return interrupts it.

context: The Bible says, so I came to Jerusalem. I was there three days.
point_summary: Before acting on the God-given burden in his heart, Nehemiah spent three days in Jerusalem in silence, illustrating that divine compulsion to serve precedes and shapes the work itself.

context: And I arose in the night, I and some few men with me. Neither told I any man what my God had put in my heart.
point_summary: God's call to service often begins as a private burden placed in the heart before it becomes a public work, as Nehemiah kept silent about what God had put in him to do at Jerusalem.

context: then said I unto them, ye see the distress that we are in, and how Jerusalem lieth waste, and the gates thereof are burned with fire. Come and let us build up the wall of Jerusalem, that we be no more a reproach.
point_summary: Nehemiah's call to rebuild Jerusalem's broken walls illustrates that God-given burdens lead to difficult, tangible labor requiring the enlistment of others to accomplish the work.

context: then I told them of the hand of my God, which was good upon me, as also the king's words that he had spoken unto me. And they said, notice what they said, let us rise up and build.
point_summary: When Nehemiah shared how God's hand had been upon him, the people responded with immediate commitment — "let us rise up and build" — illustrating that God-given vision, communicated faithfully, stirs others to join hard work for God.

context: they which build in the wall, and they that bear burdens with those that laid it, every one with one of his hands wrought in the work, and with the other hand held a weapon
point_summary: Nehemiah's wall-builders, who worked with a tool in one hand and a weapon in the other, illustrate the kind of hard, persevering labor that refuses to let enemy opposition cause God's work to cease.

context: Nehemiah chapter two verses eleven through twenty will be the text for the service this evening.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Nehemiah chapter two verses eleven through twenty will be the text for the service this evening.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Bible says in verse number two that Hanani, one of my brethren, came, he and certain men of Judah, and I asked them concerning the Jews that had escaped, which were left of the captivity, and concerning Jerusalem.
point_summary: Nehemiah's deliberate inquiry about Jerusalem's people and city reveals that burden follows intentional concern—he asked, and the troubling report he received moved him deeply.

context: and it says to us in verse number three, and they said unto me, and this is where Nehemiah receives the report, he
point_summary: Nehemiah's receipt of the devastating report—Jerusalem's walls broken, gates burned, people in affliction—is the burden-producing moment that sets the entire narrative of God-given vision in motion.

context: And the king, according to verse number two, said this is nothing more than sorrow of heart.
point_summary: The king's observation that Nehemiah's visible sadness was "sorrow of heart" shows how the burden God had placed on Nehemiah was outwardly evident before his request was ever spoken.

context: the king said unto me, For what dost thou make request?
point_summary: Before making his request to the king, Nehemiah prayed to God first, modeling the pattern of seeking divine guidance before acting on what God had placed in his heart.

context: If it please the king, and if thy servant have found favor in thy sight, that thou wouldst send me unto Judah, unto the city of my father's sepulchres, that I may build it.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: So I came to Jerusalem,
point_summary: Nehemiah's three-day arrival in Jerusalem before any action models the principle that God-given burdens require a season of quiet, undisclosed preparation before public movement.

context: and was there three days, and I rose in the night. I and some few men with me, neither told I any man what my God had put in my heart to do at Jerusalem.
point_summary: Before acting publicly on his God-given burden, Nehemiah spent three days in Jerusalem in quiet, undisclosed assessment, keeping his mission private until the right moment.

context: Neither was there any beast with me, save the beast that I rode upon, and I went out by night by the gate of the valley, even before the dragon well, and to the dung port, and viewed the walls of Jerusalem, which were broken down, and the gates thereof were consumed with fire.
point_summary: Before sharing his mission publicly, Nehemiah kept silent about what God had placed in his heart, highlighting that divine burdens are first personal and private before they become public action.

context: Then I went on to the gate of the fountain, to the king's pool, but there was no place for the beast that was under me to pass.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Then went I up in the night by the brook, and viewed the wall, and turned back, and entered by the gate of the valley, and so returned.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We'll continue reading through verse number 20 as we get through the message this evening, but I want you to notice a phrase in verse number 12, and I've used this phrase really for the sermon this evening. Notice the Bible says, and I arose in the night, in verse number 12,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I arose in the night in verse number 12. He says he took a few men with him.
point_summary: God's call placed privately in Nehemiah's heart preceded any public disclosure, as Nehemiah surveyed Jerusalem's ruins by night before telling anyone what he intended to do.

context: And notice in verse number 13, it says, and I went out by night by the gate of the valley, even before the dragon well into the dung port and viewed the walls of Jerusalem, which were broken down and the gates thereof were consumed with fire.
point_summary: Nehemiah's nighttime inspection of Jerusalem's broken walls marks the moment he personally witnessed the devastation he had only previously heard reported.

context: And then in verse 15, he went up in the night by the brook and viewed the wall and turned back and entered by the gate of the valley and so returned.
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:15 marks the completion of his nighttime reconnaissance, in which he moved from secondhand report to firsthand witness of Jerusalem's broken condition.

context: He had received a report according to chapter number one. And the report, as I read a moment ago, was that the people were in great affliction and reproach and that the wall of Jerusalem was broken down and the gates thereof are burned with fire.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: and the rulers knew not whither I went or what I did, neither had I as yet told it to the Jews, nor to the priests, nor to the nobles, nor to the rulers, nor to the rest that did the work.
point_summary: Nehemiah's deliberate silence before revealing God's burden—telling no one until he had surveyed the ruins himself—underscores that firsthand witness deepened both his conviction and his capacity to call others to action.

context: Then said I unto them, notice what he says, ye see the distress that we are in, how Jerusalem lieth waste and the gates thereof are burned with fire. Come and let us build up the wall of Jerusalem that we be no more a reproach.
point_summary: Nehemiah's call to rebuild confronts a community grown numb to their own disgrace, challenging those who had normalized the broken walls and burned gates to see their condition as the reproach it truly was.

context: so the wall in Nehemiah six and verse 15 was finished in the twenty and fifth day of the month Elul in fifty and two days.
point_summary: Collective obedience to a God-given vision produces what no individual could achieve alone, as Jerusalem's wall—completed in fifty-two days—demonstrates what God accomplishes through a unified people.

context: come and let us build up the wall of Jerusalem that we be no more reproached.
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:17's call to "rise up and build" is shown to be the launching point of a communal, laborious work that God ultimately brought to completion in fifty-two days.

context: Notice in verse 16. 16 of chapter 6, the Bible says in it came to pass that when all our enemies heard thereof and all the heathen that were about us saw these things, they were much cast down in their own eyes, notice, for they perceived that this work was wrought of our God.
point_summary: When enemies perceive that a completed work was wrought of God, it signals that the accomplishment belongs to God, not human effort—a pattern the sermon applies to gospel witness today.

context: this little ridicule in chapter number two in verse 19 is light compared to what will be faced
point_summary: Opposition to God's called work often begins with ridicule and accusation, as Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem's mocking laughter represents an early, lighter stage of resistance that will intensify.

context: in chapter number four in particular, you see how the enemy mocks and how they threaten
point_summary: Nehemiah 4 is cited as evidence that opposition to God-given work escalates—from the light mockery of chapter 2 into intensified threats and schemes—warning that resistance grows as the work advances.

context: when you get to chapter number six and we read already about him saying, Nehemiah saying, no, I'm not going to go because I'm doing a great work
point_summary: Resistance to God-given work escalates progressively—from the light mockery of Nehemiah 2 to the deceptive, relentless schemes of Nehemiah 6, where enemies used lies and false counsel to ensnare Nehemiah.

context: how Nehemiah answered this resistance that first came in verse 19
point_summary: Resistance to God's work is inevitable, but Nehemiah's response in verse 19 models faith-driven resolve: deflecting opposition by declaring that God will prosper the work.

context: In verse 20, the Bible says, then answered I them. Notice what Nehemiah said. The God of heaven, he will prosper us.
point_summary: When God's work faces opposition, Nehemiah's response in verse 20 models a faith-driven resolve: declaring that the God of heaven will prosper the work and pressing forward regardless of resistance.

context: I just want to note one statement in verse number 5.
point_summary: Even among those committed to God's work, some nobles withheld full participation, serving as a warning that internal disengagement can accompany external resistance.

context: I preached from Nehemiah chapter number one and dealt with the report that was received and the response to the report.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I'll begin in the sixth chapter, but we will be looking at a number of verses through the book of Nehemiah this evening.
point_summary: Nehemiah's account of rebuilding amid opposition illustrates the broader biblical pattern that those who pursue God-given work will face adversity and resistance.

context: But I also find that as we seek to fulfill the task, the work that God has given us to do, not only can we face the adversity and the opposition, but the reality is that we as individuals, we can grow weary, we can grow weary. And we'll look at some verses in the New Testament with regard to growing weary as we near the end of the message this evening. But I want you to notice in Nehemiah six and verse number nine
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer "strengthen my hands" frames the entire message: those doing God's work will face adversity and weariness, yet can cry out to God for renewed strength to continue.

context: And I arose in the night, I and some few men with me, neither told I any man what my God had put in my heart to do at Jerusalem. Neither was there any beast with me, save the beast that I rode upon.
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:12 establishes that the burden to rebuild Jerusalem's walls originated with God, not human initiative—Nehemiah himself attributed the mission entirely to what God had put in his heart.

context: And I sent messengers unto them, saying, I am doing a great work, so that I cannot come down. Why should the work cease whilst I leave it and come down to you?
point_summary: Nehemiah's refusal to descend from the wall rests on his conviction that the work was a great work — a designation the sermon grounds in the greatness of God himself, not merely the scale of the task.

context: But we find that as we look in Nehemiah 4, notice in verse number seven, it says, but it came to pass that when Sanballat and Tobiah and the Arabians and the Ammonites and the Ashtodites heard that the walls of Jerusalem were made up
point_summary: When opposition to God's work intensifies and multiplies—as the coalition of enemies in Nehemiah 4:7 illustrates—the proper response is prayer combined with practical vigilance, not abandonment of the work.

context: For they all made us afraid, saying, their hands should be weakened from the work that it be not done.
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer "strengthen my hands" arose from his recognition that the enemy's explicit goal was to weaken the workers' hands and halt the work, and that only God could counter that strategy.

context: now therefore, oh God, strengthen my hands.
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer "strengthen my hands" arose from his recognition that the enemy's explicit goal was to weaken the workers' hands and halt the work, and that only God's ability could counter that strategy.

context: when he says, oh God, strengthen my hands,
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer "strengthen my hands" is presented as a model of asking rightly — specific, appropriate to the moment, and free from the self-serving motives James 4 warns against.

context: But we come to verse number 15 and 16 of Nehemiah 6, and I want you to see what the Bible says.
point_summary: The completion of the wall in verse 15 is presented as the direct answer to Nehemiah's prayer for strengthened hands, demonstrating that God hears and answers when His people ask.

context: But we come to verse number 15 and 16 of Nehemiah 6, and I want you to see what the Bible says.
point_summary: When God answers prayer, the enemy's goal of weakening the workers' hands is defeated, and the work is completed — as Nehemiah 6:15–16 demonstrates with the wall's finishing.

context: The Bible tells us in the fourth chapter of the book of Nehemiah, in verse number 17, that everyone with one of his hands wrought in the work and with the other hand held a weapon.
point_summary: Nehemiah's workers labored under constant threat, building with one hand while holding a weapon with the other, yet the wall was still completed in 52 days despite the enemy's unrelenting opposition.

context: Notice in verse number 16, it says, and it came to pass that when all our enemies heard thereof and all the heathen that were about us saw these things, they were much cast down in their own eyes. And then notice the statement at the end of verse number 16, for they perceived that this work was wrought of our God.
point_summary: When enemies and onlookers witnessed the wall's completion in 52 days, even they were compelled to acknowledge that the work had been accomplished by God—vindicating Nehemiah's labor against all opposition.

context: We find that that's exactly what the enemy recognized in Nehemiah 6 and verse number 16.
point_summary: When the wall was completed, even the enemies recognized in Nehemiah 6:16 that the work had been done by God—confirming that God-empowered work cannot be overthrown.

context: The Bible tells us as to the day in which the work was completed. In verse number 15 of Nehemiah 6,
point_summary: The completion of the wall on the 25th of Elul is cited as the historical marker establishing when God's work was finished, bridging directly to the corporate worship and Scripture reading of Nehemiah 8.

context: the wall was completed, according to Nehemiah chapter six
point_summary: The completion of the wall on the 25th day of Elul (Neh. 6:15) sets the precise chronological backdrop for the public reading of the Law just six days later in Nehemiah 8.

context: And it says in verse number three, and he read therein before the street that was before the water gate from the morning until midday
point_summary: Just six days after completing the wall, the same people who had labored exhaustingly for 52 days stood attentively from morning until midday listening to the public reading of God's law.

context: And Nehemiah in chapter number six says, oh God, strengthen my hands.
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer for strengthened hands in chapter six finds its fulfillment six days after the wall's completion, as those same hands are lifted in worship before God.

context: And here we are six days later, and the Bible tells us in verse number four, and Ezra the scribe stood upon a pulpit of wood, which they had made for the purpose.
point_summary: The hands strengthened for Nehemiah's wall-building labor are now, six days after its completion, raised in worship before the open Word—the same hands that worked now extended in adoration to God.

context: And Ezra opened the book in the sight of all the people for he was above all the people. And when he opened it, all the people stood up
point_summary: The hands strengthened for Nehemiah's wall-building labor are, six days later, the same hands lifted in worship, showing that God-given strength for work culminates in adoration.

context: And Ezra blessed the Lord, the great God. And all the people answered, amen, amen.
point_summary: The hands strengthened for the wall's construction are now raised in worship, showing that God-given strength for labor finds its culmination in adoration of Him.

context: and the seed of Israel separated themselves from all the strangers, notice this, and stood and confessed their sins and the iniquities of their fathers.
point_summary: Nehemiah 9:2 illustrates a pattern where reading God's Word exposes sin, prompting confession — the people spent a quarter of the day reading and another quarter confessing what the Word revealed.

context: And we find that it's said in Nehemiah 9.17 that God is a God who is ready to pardon.
point_summary: Nehemiah 9:17's declaration that God is "ready to pardon" grounds the petition "forgive us our sins" in God's very character and nature as one who is always willing to forgive.

context: and what a wonderful account, Nehemiah chapter 8 records that for us, and I mean just an exciting passage of Scripture
point_summary: Nehemiah 8's account of Ezra standing on a wooden pulpit to teach God's Word is cited as the celebrated moment of public ministry that his earlier preparation in Ezra 7 made possible.

context: and all the people gathered themselves together as one man into the street that was before the water gate and they spake unto Ezra the scribe to bring the book of the law of Moses which the Lord had commanded to Israel
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:1 is cited to show that Ezra's prior preparation—seeking, doing, and teaching the law—positioned him as the one the people turned to when they gathered and demanded the Word of God.

context: the people gathered themselves together I hope in your mind's eye you can see this gathering in Nehemiah chapter number 8 and the Bible tells us that they spake unto Ezra and they said to Ezra who was the scribe they said bring the book of the law of Moses
point_summary: The people's request that Ezra bring the book of the law confirms that his years of preparation to seek, do, and teach God's Word had made him the recognized authority for public Scripture reading.

context: notice in verse 2 and Ezra the priest brought the law before the congregation both of men and women and all that could hear with understanding upon the first day of the seventh month
point_summary: Ezra's priestly reading of the law before the entire congregation—men, women, and all who could understand—from morning until midday illustrates the attentiveness God's people are to bring to hearing his Word.

context: In verse number 8, the Bible says, so they read in the book in the law of God distinctly and gave the sense and caused them to understand the reading.
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:8 is presented as a three-part model for preaching: reading the Word distinctly, giving its sense through exposition, and causing the people to understand it.

context: ...Ezra the scribe that he's at this place and he's doing the work that we find here in Nehemiah chapter number 8...
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:8 is presented as a model for preaching: reading Scripture distinctly, giving its sense through exposition, and causing the people to understand it.

context: ...when they spake unto Ezra and said bring the book of the law of Moses...
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:1's call for Ezra to bring the law and teach illustrates that because Ezra had already prepared his heart, he was ready when the opportunity came.

context: then I came to the governors beyond the river and gave them the king's letters. Now the king had sent captains of the army and horsemen with me.
point_summary: Nehemiah's escort of army captains and horsemen is cited as a contrast to Ezra's deliberate choice to trust God alone, showing that requesting protection would not have been inherently wrong.

context: and it came to pass in the month Nisan in the 20th year of Artaxerxes the king
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:1 establishes the precise starting point of Daniel's 70-weeks clock by recording the exact month and regnal year of Artaxerxes—historically dateable to 444 BC—when Nehemiah appeared before the king.

context: wine was before him, the latter part of verse two, and I took up the wine and gave it unto the king
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:2 anchors the decree to rebuild Jerusalem to a precise, datable moment—the month Nisan in Artaxerxes' 20th year (444 BC)—establishing the unambiguous starting point for Daniel's seventy-week prophecy.

context: now I had not been before time sad in his presence
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:2 anchors the prophetic countdown of Daniel's seventy weeks to a datable historical moment—the month Nisan in the 20th year of Artaxerxes (444 BC)—establishing the precise starting point of the decree with no ambiguity or speculation required.

context: the king said unto me, why is thy countenance sad? Seeing thou art not sick, this is nothing else but sorrow of heart
point_summary: Nehemiah 2:2 anchors the decree to rebuild Jerusalem to a precise, datable moment—the month Nisan in Artaxerxes' 20th year (444 BC)—establishing the unambiguous starting point for Daniel's seventy-week prophecy.

context: Then I was sore afraid and said unto the king, let the king live forever. Why should not my countenance be sad when the city, the place of my father's sepulchres, lieth waste and the gates thereof are consumed with fire?
point_summary: Nehemiah's grief before Artaxerxes in the month Nisan, 20th year of his reign, provides the precisely datable starting point—444 BC—for the decree that triggers Daniel's seventy-week countdown.

context: Verse four, then the king said unto me, for what dost thou make request? So I prayed to the God of heaven
point_summary: The king's question in Nehemiah 2:4 marks the precise historical moment—444 BC, the month Nisan in Artaxerxes' 20th year—that triggers the decree starting Daniel's 70-week countdown.

context: as you look back to chapter number one, as I mentioned a moment ago, that he was inquiring specifically at the end of verse number two concerning Jerusalem
point_summary: Nehemiah 1:2 establishes that Nehemiah's inquiry was specifically concerning Jerusalem, providing the historical context that anchors the 444 BC starting point for Daniel's seventy weeks.

context: Nehemiah standing before Artaxerxes makes the request in verse number five
point_summary: Nehemiah's request to Artaxerxes in 444 BC, granted with letters and timber, establishes the historical starting point for the decree that initiates Daniel's seventy weeks.

context: we read in verse number six that the king said unto me, the queen also sitting by him, for how long shall thy journey be?
point_summary: Nehemiah's request before Artaxerxes in 444 BC establishes the historical starting point — the decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem — from which Daniel's seventy weeks are calculated.

context: And so we find as we read here in Nehemiah chapter two
point_summary: Nehemiah 2 records Artaxerxes granting Nehemiah's request to rebuild Jerusalem, identifying this decree as the starting point of Daniel 9:25's prophetic clock.

context: the issuing of the commandment was given as we read there in Nehemiah chapter two
point_summary: Nehemiah 2's account of Artaxerxes granting the commandment to restore Jerusalem marks the precise starting point of Daniel's 69 weeks—the first seven of which are fulfilled in the rebuilding described in Nehemiah 4–6.

context: I don't have time this evening to read the entirety of Nehemiah chapter four, five and six
point_summary: Nehemiah 4–6 fulfills Daniel 9:25's phrase "built again in troublous times," marking the completion of the first seven weeks (49 years) from the commandment's issuance in Nehemiah 2.

context: I don't have time this evening to read the entirety of Nehemiah chapter four, five and six
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I don't have time this evening to read the entirety of Nehemiah chapter four, five and six
point_summary: Nehemiah 4–6 fulfills Daniel 9:25's description of Jerusalem's wall being rebuilt "in troublous times," marking the completion of the prophetic clock's first seven weeks (49 years).

context: start at the place that we spoke of in Nehemiah, four forty four B.C.
point_summary: The 444 B.C. date of Nehemiah's completed wall marks the prophetic starting point from which 483 years (69 weeks) count forward to 30 A.D. and Messiah's crucifixion.

context: we'll look at the book of Nehemiah here in just a little while
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And all the people gathered themselves together as one man into the street that was before the water gate. And they spake unto Ezra the scribe to bring the book of the law of Moses, which the Lord had commanded to Israel.
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:1 is cited as a prominent biblical example of the public reading of God's Word, where the gathered people collectively requested that Ezra bring and read the law before them.

context: brought the law before the congregation, both of men and women and all that could hear with understanding, upon the first day of the seventh month. And he read therein before the street that was before the water gate.
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:2 is cited to illustrate the public, congregational reading of God's Word—from morning until midday—as a biblical precedent for sustained, attentive engagement with Scripture regardless of modern demands for brevity.

context: And the ears of all the people were attentive unto the book of the law.
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:3's record of the people's attentiveness from morning until midday is cited as biblical evidence that lengthy engagement with God's Word is not unreasonable, countering modern assumptions about short attention spans.

context: He says in verse number four, and Ezra the scribe stood upon a pulpit of wood
point_summary: Ezra's construction of a wooden pulpit specifically for public Scripture reading illustrates that the formal, elevated proclamation of God's Word is a purposeful, prepared act—not incidental.

context: And Ezra opened the book in the sight of all the people for he was above all the people and when he opened it all the people stood up
point_summary: When Ezra opened the book and all the people stood, their reverent, attentive posture—from morning until midday—challenges the modern claim that congregations cannot sustain long, substantive engagement with God's Word.

context: Verse 8, so they read in the book in the law of God distinctly and gave the sense and caused them to understand the reading
point_summary: Nehemiah 8:8's description of reading Scripture "distinctly" and giving "the sense" grounds the call for systematic, purposeful engagement with God's Word rather than casual, headline-skimming reading.

context: if you read Nehemiah 1 we won't take the time to go there but Nehemiah is praying
point_summary: Nehemiah's brief in-the-moment prayer before Artaxerxes illustrates that God answers both extended, laboring prayer and short, spontaneous petitions offered in urgent circumstances.

context: We come to verse number 11. The Bible says, so I came to Jerusalem and was there three days.
point_summary: Nehemiah's quiet, unannounced arrival in Jerusalem illustrates that God's work in a heart is often carried out in private, before any public action or disclosure.

context: In verse number 12, notice what the Bible says, I arose in the night, I and some few men with me.
point_summary: Obedience and sensitivity to God's inward promptings are prerequisite to continued divine leading, illustrated by Nehemiah acting quietly on what God had placed in his heart before disclosing it to anyone.

context: neither told I any man what my God had put in my heart to do at Jerusalem. Neither was there any beast with me save the beast that I rode upon
point_summary: God's inward work precedes outward action: Nehemiah kept silent about what God had put in his heart until the right moment, demonstrating that divine calling is first a private, personal conviction before it becomes a public mission.

context: then I said unto them, ye see the distress that we are in, how Jerusalem lieth waste and the gates thereof are burned with fire
point_summary: Nehemiah's disclosure in verse 17—naming Jerusalem's ruin and calling the people to rebuild—was the moment he revealed what God had privately placed in his heart, demonstrating that divine burden moves from inward conviction to outward action.

context: then I told them of the hand of my God which was good upon me, as also the king's words that he had spoken unto me, and they said, let us rise up and build
point_summary: When Nehemiah disclosed to the people that God's good hand was upon him, it was the testimony of divine initiative—God had already placed the burden in his heart before any human mobilization began.

context: he told them in verse 18, of the hand of my God which was good upon me
point_summary: Nehemiah's testimony that the hand of God was good upon him confirmed that the divine compulsion placed in his heart was not self-generated but traceable to God's sovereign working.

context: He had referenced that in verse number 8, according to the good hand of my God upon me
point_summary: Nehemiah's repeated acknowledgment of "the good hand of my God upon me" establishes that divine enablement, not personal ambition, was the recognized source of his burden and capacity to act.

context: and the king granted me according to the good hand of my God upon me
point_summary: Nehemiah's declaration that "the good hand of my God" was upon him illustrates that divine favor is not accidental but flows from seeking, obeying, and brokenness before God.

context: after he had wept and poured his heart out to God in Nehemiah chapter one
point_summary: Nehemiah's weeping, confession, and brokenness before God in chapter one is presented as the foundation that explains the divine favor visibly granted to him in chapter two.

context: there was just a brokenness in the life of Nehemiah in chapter number one and you see God's favor in his life
point_summary: Nehemiah's weeping and confession before God in chapter one is presented as the foundation of the divine favor visibly rewarded in chapter two, illustrating that God's blessing follows brokenness and obedience.

context: but they and our fathers dealt proudly and hardened their necks and hearkened not to thy commandments
point_summary: Despite Israel's repeated pride, stiff-necked rebellion, and refusal to obey, God's response—as the very next verse declares—was pardon, mercy, and faithfulness rather than abandonment.

context: refuse to obey, and refuse to obey, neither were mindful of thy wonders that thou didst among them, but hardened their necks, and in their rebellion appointed a captain to return to their bondage
point_summary: Despite Israel's proud rebellion and hardened refusal to obey, God remained ready to pardon, gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and did not forsake them — demonstrating that God is not like us.

context: but thou art a God ready to pardon, gracious and merciful, slow to anger and of great kindness, and forsookest them not
point_summary: Even amid Israel's repeated rebellion and hardened refusal to obey, God remained ready to pardon, gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and did not forsake them.

context: therefore thou deliverest them into the hand of their enemies who vexed them, and in the time of their trouble
point_summary: Even in Israel's repeated cycles of rebellion and bondage, God's manifold mercies moved Him to hear their cries and raise up deliverers — demonstrating that He answers when His people call in trouble.

context: if you read through Nehemiah nine
point_summary: Nehemiah 9's rehearsal of Israel's repeated rebellion and God's repeated deliverance through the judges illustrates divine long-suffering: even when his people refused obedience, God heard their cries and raised up saviors according to his manifold mercies.

context: in Nehemiah chapter number one, look at the example, a positive example in the life of Nehemiah
point_summary: Nehemiah's response to a discouraging report about Jerusalem—turning immediately to prayer—is presented as a parallel exemplary model of faith alongside Moses's cry for Miriam's healing.

context: and said, I beseech thee, O Lord, God of heaven, the great and terrible God that keepeth covenant and mercy for them that love him and observe his commandments, let thine ear now be attentive and thine eyes open that thou mayest hear the prayer of thy servant
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer of corporate confession—owning sin personally alongside his people—is presented as a model of genuine repentance, where intercession begins with humble identification rather than distance from the transgression.

context: I beseech thee, the word that thou commandest thy servant, Moses, saying, if you transgress, I will scatter you abroad among the nations
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer appeals to God's own word to Moses — that Israel's scattering among the nations was the direct consequence of their disobedience — grounding his intercession in God's covenant faithfulness rather than Israel's merit.

context: but if you turn unto me and keep my commandments and do them, though there were of you cast out under the uttermost part of the heaven, yet will I gather them from thence and will bring them unto the place that I have chosen to set my name there
point_summary: God's promise of restoration in Nehemiah 1:9 — that even the most scattered exiles will be regathered if they return and obey — forms the scriptural ground on which Nehemiah bases his intercessory prayer for Jerusalem.

context: Nehemiah chapter 1, Nehemiah is praying
point_summary: Nehemiah's prayer in chapter 1 models corporate repentance by confessing both personal and ancestral sin, grounding its appeal in God's own conditional promise through Moses that disobedience brings exile but return brings restoration.

context: I was reading through the book of Nehemiah
point_summary: Affliction, as seen in Israel's experience in Nehemiah, can function as a corrective that turns the wandering heart back to keeping God's word.

context: I was reading the book of Nehemiah and Esther this morning
point_summary: Nehemiah's record of Esther beseeching the king with tears is cited as warrant for believers to approach God with tears in urgent prayer.
